Package io.simonis.jballoon

Class JBalloon

java.lang.Object
io.simonis.jballoon.JBalloon
public class JBalloon extends Object
A utility class which allows Java code to instantly release a part of the Java heap memory to the OS and reclaim it back whenever necessary. Releasing memory from the Java heap to the OS (i.e. "inflating" a balloon) happens instantly while re-allocation that memory back to the Java heap (i.e. "deflating" a balloon) happens lazily.

It is possible to create an arbitrary number of inflated balloons as long as there is enough unused memory in the Java heap.

The intended use cases for JBalloon are applications where the JVM is running with a large Java heap which consumes almost all of the available native memory, but from time to time they either have to allocate a significant amount of native, off-heap memory (e.g. through NIO ByteBuffers), use the JNI or Foreign Function & Memory API with a high native memory demand or have to execute native sub-programs which themselves require a considerable amount of native memory either directly or e.g. through Project Detroit or Project Babylon.

The name JBalloon is inspired by the Virtual I/O Device (VIRTIO)'s Memory Balloon Device. Basic usage of JBalloon looks as follows: 


 JBalloon.Balloon balloon;
 JBalloon jBalloon = JBalloon.getInstance();
 if (jBalloon != null) {
     // Try to release 128mb heap memory back to the OS.
     balloon = jBalloon.inflate(128*1024*1024);
     if (balloon != null) {
         // Now we returned 128mb from the Java Heap back to the OS.
         ... // Execute some native code.
         jBalloon.deflate(balloon);
         // At this point Java can re-use the 128mb again.
     }
 }

    JBalloon.Balloon
    A lightweight handle class for managing memory balloons.

  • Method Details

    • getInstance

      public static JBalloon getInstance()
      Initializes and returns the singleton JBalloon instance. If JBalloon can't be initialized, null will be returned. In such cases you can enable the DEBUG log level of JBalloon's native library by setting the environment variable LOG=DEBUG to get more information about why the initialization failed.
      Returns:
      the JBalloon singleton instance or null if JBalloon can't be initialized.

    • inflate

      public JBalloon.Balloon inflate(long size) throws OutOfMemoryError
      Creates and inflates a new JBalloon.Balloon with size rounded up to such that it spans at least one system page size (i.e. getconf PAGE_SIZE) and fills up all the GC regions it spans.

      The Balloon is backed up by a long[] so the balloon can't get larger than 8 * Integer.MAX_VALUE bytes.

      The JBalloon.Balloon.heapSize() and JBalloon.Balloon.balloonSize() methods can be used to query the actual size in bytes of the backing array and the size of the inflated balloon.

      If the Balloon can't be created ot inflated, this method returns null, otherwise the newly created Balloon is returned.

      Parameters:
      size - the size in bytes of the new JBalloon.Balloon.
      Returns:
      the new Balloon or null if a Balloon of size size couldn't be created or inflated.
      Throws:
      OutOfMemoryError - if the backing long[] can't be allocated.

    • inflate

      public JBalloon.Balloon inflate(long[] array)
      Creates and inflates a new JBalloon.Balloon backed up by array.

      Once this method returns, array shouldn't be used any more because its content can be overwritten at any time. Also, writing into array after this method returns will deflate parts of the created Balloon.

      The recommended usage pattern for this method is to only use arrays as input argument which have been returned by deflate(Balloon).

      The JBalloon.Balloon.balloonSize() method can be used to query the the actual size in bytes of the inflated balloon.

      If the Balloon can't be inflated or if the array is too small, this method returns null, otherwise the newly created Balloon is returned.

      Parameters:
      array - a long[] to back up the new Balloon.
      Returns:
      the new Balloon or null if the array is too small or the Balloon couldn't be inflated.

    • deflate

      public long[] deflate(JBalloon.Balloon b)
      Deflates the Balloon b and returns the backing long[].

      This method doesn't deflate b instantly. Instead, deflation will only happen when the GC collects the returned long[]. This won't happen as long as the returned array is kept alive by the application.

      The recommended usage pattern for this method is to either ignore the returned long[] or to store it into a WeakHashMap if the application expects to inflate a new Balloon of similar size any time soon. In the latter case, the array can be retrieved from the WeakHashMap if it hasn't been collected by the GC and passed as argument to inflate(long[]).

      Parameters:
      b - the Balloon which will be deflated.
      Returns:
      the backing long[] array of b or null if b was already deflated.

    • deflateAll

      public void deflateAll()
      Deflates all currently active Balloons.

    • balloonHeapSize

      public long balloonHeapSize()
      Returns the size in bytes occupied by all the long[] which back up active (i.e. inflated) Balloons. This is typically larger than balloonSize().
      Returns:
      the size in bytes of all backing long[].

    • balloonSize

      public long balloonSize()
      Returns the size in bytes of all active (i.e. inflated) Balloons. This is usually smaller than balloonHeapSize().
      Returns:
      the size in bytes of all inflated Balloons.

    • regionSize

      public static long regionSize()
      Returns the region size of the current Garbage Collector.
      Returns:
      the GC's region size in bytes.

    • objectHeaderSize

      public static long objectHeaderSize()
      Returns the object header size of a Java object.
      Returns:
      the object header size in bytes.

    • pageSize

      public static long pageSize()
      Returns the default system page size (i.e. getconf PAGE_SIZE) in bytes.
      Returns:
      the default system page size in bytes.

    • heapSize

      public static long heapSize()
      Returns the Java object heap size in bytes.
      Returns:
      the heap size size in bytes.

    • heapBase

      public static long heapBase()
      Returns the base address of the Java object heap.
      Returns:
      the Java heap's base address.

    • longArrayOffset

      public static long longArrayOffset()
      Returns the offset in bytes of the first element in a long[] relatively to the arrays address in the Java heap. This includes the objectHeaderSize() and potential alignment gaps.
      Returns:
      the offset in bytes of the first long[] element relatively to the long[] object.

    • mincoreHeapSize

      public static long mincoreHeapSize()
      Returns the size in bytes occupied by the Java heap as returned by the mincore(2) C-library call. Notice that mincore() result counts each page in the heap that is backed up by the kernel's zero page as "resident" although such pages don't actually consume any physical pages.
      Returns:
      the heap size in bytes as counted by mincore().

    • rssHeapSize

      public static long rssHeapSize()
      Returns the size in bytes occupied by the Java heap as accounted for in the /proc/self/pagemap file. This doesn't account for zero pages in the heap and is therefore more accurate than mincoreHeapSize().
      Returns:
      the heap size in bytes as accounted for in /proc/self/pagemap.
